title: The Philistines in the Highlands: A View from Ashkelon (Jerusalem Journal of Archaeology 1, pp. 203–220)
author: Daniel M. Master
allowedUseNotes: Copyrighted journal article. Summaries in project's own words only.
summary: Argues, from combined biblical redaction-history and archaeological evidence, that Philistines were active in the central highlands as opportunistic raiders (not an occupying army) in the late Iron I–early Iron IIA window, as Mediterranean trade collapse pressured coastal Philistine cities. Relevant to Gilboa as independent support that a Philistine presence/raiding force in or near the highlands in roughly the right chronological window (late 11th–early 10th century BCE) is a defensible scholarly position — but the article argues for Philistine highland activity generally, not for the feathered-headdress motif specifically; it contains no claim that the headdress itself is attested in a highland or inland findspot.
confidenceNotes: Bibliographic details (journal, volume, page range 203–220, author) and the article's argument confirmed via corroborating search-engine abstract/summary results; direct PDF fetch returned HTTP 403, so this card is built from abstract-level material, not a full read. TO VERIFY: the article's specific engagement (if any) with material-culture markers versus its predominantly textual/settlement-pattern argument, and whether it discusses the Gilboa narrative by name.
